sanskrit_devanagari:
मत्तः परतरं नान्यत्किञ्चिदस्ति धनञ्जय ।
मयि सर्वमिदं प्रोतं सूत्रे मणिगणा इव ॥ ७ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
mattaḥ parataraṁ nānyat kiñcid asti dhanañjaya |
mayi sarvam idaṁ protaṁ sūtre maṇi-gaṇā iva || 7 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical).
exact (academic edition; text body identical; punctuation/danda rendering differs).
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_07.007 = MBh_06,029.007). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: O conqueror of wealth, there is no truth superior to Me. Everything rests upon Me, as pearls are strung on a thread.
There is nothing higher than Myself, O Arjun. Everything rests in Me, as beads strung on a thread.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: There is a common controversy over whether the Supreme Absolute Truth is personal or impersonal. As far as Bhagavad-gītā is concerned, the Absolute Truth is the Personality of Godhead, Śrī Kṛṣṇa, and this is confirmed in every step.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.7 O Dhanañjaya, asti, there is; na anyat kiñcit, nothing else whatsoever, no other cause; parataram, higher; mattaḥ, than Me, the supreme God; i.e. I Myself am the source of the world. Since this is so, therefore, sarvam, all; idam, this, all things, the Universe; protam, is strung, woven, connected.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads parataram as 'higher cause' — there is no other cause higher than Krishna. The simile of beads-on-thread illustrates that all things are strung, woven, connected on Krishna as the underlying causal substrate. The thread is what holds the beads together; pull the thread, the beads scatter.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.7 I am absolutely superior to all in two ways: 1) I am the cause of both the Prakṛtis and I am also their controlling master (Seṣin). This controllership over inanimate nature is exercised through the animate Prakṛti (the Jīvas) who form the inner controller of their bodies.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads parataram on two axes: causality (Krishna is the cause of both prakṛtis) and lordship (Krishna is the Śeṣin, the controlling master). The beads-on-thread image fits the Vishishtadvaita doctrine of antaryāmin — Krishna as inner controller threading through every being.

positions.rendering: Everything rests upon Me, as pearls are strung on a thread (maṇi as pearls)
Everything rests in Me, as beads strung on a thread (maṇi as beads)
all things strung, woven, connected on Krishna as the underlying causal substrate
the antaryāmin reading — Krishna threads through every being as inner controller

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Maṇi can mean pearl, gem, or bead — translators choose differently. The simile's structural force is identical across translations: a string of beads holds together only because of the thread running through them; the thread is what makes the string a string, not a scatter. Shankara reads this as causal-substrate (the thread = the cause that grounds all effects); Ramanuja reads it as antaryāmin (the thread = Krishna as inner controller threading through every being). Engineering layers must honor BOTH halves of the simile: the thread is prior to the beads (without thread, no string); the beads are real (the thread without beads is not the universe).

literal_meaning: There is nothing higher than Me, O Dhanañjaya. All this is strung on Me as beads on a thread.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The chapter's central image. Krishna names himself as the unique highest reality (parataraṁ nānyat) and gives the simile that survives every commentary tradition: all things are strung on him as beads are strung on a thread. Two structural truths follow: (1) the thread is what makes the beads a string — without it the beads scatter; (2) the thread is prior to the beads — beads change, the thread sustains. Shankara reads this as the causal substrate (kāraṇa); Ramanuja as the inner controller (antaryāmin). Both readings honor the simile's force: nothing exists in disconnection; everything that is, is held together by Krishna.
